content:
To solve this problem, let's consider the relationship between the positions of the circles and their radii.

Consider two touching circles (e.g. circles $C_{n-1}$ and $C_n$). We will call the point of tangency $Q$ and the center of the larger circle $D$. Let $r$ be the radius of the larger circle, $r'$ be the radius of the smaller circle, and $d$ be the distance between $D$ and the point of tangency $Q$ on the line connecting them. 

Then we find the $d = r-r'$.

Now let's look back at the diagram and find our point $D$ of the larger circle $B$ which is the leftmost of two tangent circles which touch a line and is the leftmost of all our series circle.

We now know from previous diagrams  that point $D$ is at distance $(1+2008) = 2009 $from line $A$. 

Also let's take a closer look at one of the $C$ circles with radius  $r$ and center $D$. Because it is tangent to line $A$, the radius of circle $C$, $r$, must be equal to the distance from point $D$ to the line.

Also we see that there are circles $C_n$, all of which is to the right of point $P$ , which means the point of tangency, $Q$, on line $A$ is also to the right of point $P$.

Using the same principle we can now write the $x$ coordinate of $C$'s center (denoted as $D_x$), where $x$ is the x-axis, is the $x$ coordinate of the point of tangency on circle $B$  ($2008 + r$),  and the  distance to the point of tangency on the line from the center of $C$ is ($r$). This results in $D_x = (2008 + r)$, so we can now set $r$ as $D_x-2008$.

The distance to the point $P$ is $2008-D_x$.

We can similarly determine the $D_x  = (2008 + (r+r'))$ for the $C_n$ circle where $r$ is now a larger radius of circle $C_2$, so using this as a pattern we have $D_x  = (2008 + (r+r'+r''))$ . Now if we repeat the above formula for our larger $C_k$ radius circle we find that the  distance of the point of $P$ with the $C_k$ circle center is $P - D_x   =  [- 2008 - r -  r' - r'' - \cdots  - r_{n-1}]$. This is less than $0$ if $n >1$, so this circle can then no longer be constructed in this way when its center would be to the left of  point $P$.
